topic: JetBrains Air Review 2026: Multi-Agent Development Environment from JetBrains

key_points: JetBrains Air positions itself as an orchestrator for multiple AI models, not just a single-model assistant
Compared directly against Cursor and GitHub Copilot for multi-agent tasks
Noted that Air was faster for cross-cutting changes across modules
Multiple simultaneous agents were a genuine productivity gain for reviewers
Recommended keeping current IDE for production; use Air alongside for agent tasks
Critical take: questions whether multi-agent orchestration is truly needed by most developers
Highlights that Air is macOS-only in preview, limiting adoption
Notes agents still struggle with code architecture and following project patterns
Acknowledges that Air fills a genuine gap between specialized agent tools and mainstream IDE functionality
Suggests Air is for power users already comfortable with multiple agent subscriptions
First-person account from a developer who adopted Air fully
Reports noticeably improved productivity from parallel task delegation
Only needed IntelliJ IDEA for debugger; Air handled everything else
Interacting with agents in a familiar environment reduced friction
Agents ran one task adding tests while another fixed a bug concurrently
Coverage of Air being built on the abandoned Fleet IDE codebase
Fleet became unavailable December 22, 2025; Air emerged from its codebase
JetBrains head of product: agents are changing how software is made
Air described as 'agentic IDE built on the corpse of abandoned Fleet'
ACP (Agent Client Protocol) co-sponsored by JetBrains and Zed editor
Summarizes Air as unifying the AI agent development ecosystem
Emphasizes 26 years of JetBrains tooling experience behind the product
Built-in code review tools differentiate Air from terminal-only agent runners
ACP agent registry allows any compliant agent to integrate
Recommends Air for teams managing complex multi-module codebases

stats.fact: JetBrains Air launched in public preview in March 2026, built on the codebase of the discontinued Fleet IDE (shutdown December 22, 2025)
11% of developers worldwide use JetBrains AI tools, with JetBrains AI Assistant used by 9% and Junie by 5%
JetBrains AI adoption grew by +101.33% quarter-over-quarter in Q2 2025 — the largest quarterly gain recorded
GitHub Copilot has 76% developer awareness and 29% active use, but growth has stalled year-over-year
GitHub Copilot scores 56% on SWE-bench; Cursor scores 51.7% — both are single-model tools Air competes with
JetBrains Air supports 4 agents natively: OpenAI Codex, Anthropic Claude Agent, Google Gemini CLI, and JetBrains Junie
Junie (JetBrains' own agent) costs from $10/month (individual) to $60/month (enterprise)
Air itself is free during the public preview period; users must supply their own agent API keys or subscriptions

angles: Multi-agent orchestration as the next paradigm shift: why running one AI model at a time is becoming obsolete
Fleet's death, Air's birth: how JetBrains pivoted from traditional IDE to agent-first environment
The ACP standard vs. MCP vs. proprietary integrations: who wins the agent protocol war?
Air as a force multiplier: can parallel agent tasks 10x developer output on complex codebases?
The hidden cost of Air: you still pay for each agent subscription separately — total cost analysis
Air for solo vs. team workflows: different value propositions explained
macOS-only limitation: why JetBrains' decision to delay Windows/Linux could cost them early adopters
JetBrains' 26-year advantage: does deep IDE tooling expertise translate into better agent orchestration?

H2: Key Features of JetBrains Air
H2: Supported AI Agents: Codex, Claude, Gemini, and Junie

H2: Pricing and Availability (macOS, Windows, Linux)
H2: Pros and Cons of JetBrains Air
H2: Who Should Use JetBrains Air in 2026?
